config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. In my view, society matters a lot, but it should not be allowed to override individual rights by default, because that is exactly how abuse and authoritarianism begin. In Indonesia, we know how easily “the common good” can be used to silence dissent or ignore minority voices, so I am very cautious about giving society priority over the individual. A healthy society protects people’s rights first, then asks individuals to accept limits only when those limits are truly necessary and fair.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. From a U.S. perspective, our legal and political culture is built around the idea that individual rights are a safeguard against the majority trampling people they find inconvenient, unpopular, or different. That said, rights are not absolute in every situation, because society does need rules for public safety and basic order, but those limits should be narrow, justified, and subject to strict scrutiny. If we flip the default and put society first, I think we make it too easy for governments or majorities to excuse oppression in the name of the common good.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe society should take priority over individual rights as a general rule. From my Indonesian perspective, the phrase “common good” can sound noble, but it is also often used to pressure minorities, silence criticism, or justify unfair treatment, and that is a real danger we should not ignore. Society absolutely needs shared rules, but those rules should protect people first and only limit individual freedom when the restriction is clearly necessary, proportionate, and open to challenge. If we reverse the default, we make it too easy for majorities or authorities to abuse power while claiming they are serving everyone.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should take priority over the rights of the individual, because in the United States that idea has too often been used to excuse majoritarian overreach. The strongest societies here are the ones that treat individual rights as the starting point, not the obstacle, while still allowing narrow limits for real public safety needs like violence, fraud, or emergencies. I also do not buy the argument that putting rights first is selfish; it is usually the best defense against powerful institutions deciding whose freedoms matter less. When society comes first by default, it becomes far easier to rationalize censorship, discrimination, and abuse in the name of the common good.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: Society should not take priority over the rights of the individual by default, and I say that very directly from an Indonesian perspective. In Indonesia, I have seen how “public interest” or “harmony” can be used to pressure people into silence, especially minorities or those who criticize authority, so I do not trust that phrase as a blank check. That said, individual rights are not absolute in every case, because public safety and social order do matter, but the burden must always be on society to prove that a restriction is necessary, proportionate, and fair. If we start by assuming society comes first, it becomes too easy to excuse abuse while calling it the common good.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: Society should not take priority over the rights of the individual by default, because in the United States that has historically been a dangerous shortcut to majoritarian abuse. I do not buy the idea that putting individual rights first is anti-social; in practice, it is often what protects unpopular people from being steamrolled by governments, mobs, or institutions claiming to represent everyone. At the same time, I am not saying rights are unlimited, because laws for public safety, fraud prevention, and emergencies are legitimate when they are narrow and accountable. The burden should always be on society to justify any restriction, not on individuals to prove they deserve basic freedom.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
